g.data-narration: Welcome to quantifiers in mathematical logic. We explore how to make statements about collections of objects using ∀ and ∃.
g.text: Universal and Existential Quantifiers in Discrete Mathematics

g.data-narration: Quantifiers let us express statements about all or some elements, forming the backbone of formal specification and verification.
g.text: Quantifiers transform predicates (open sentences) into propositions with truth values.
They are essential for: formal specifications, database queries, algorithm correctness proofs, and formal verification.
means "for all x, P(x) is true"
means "there exists x such that P(x)"

g.data-narration: There are two fundamental quantifiers: universal (∀) means 'for all', and existential (∃) means 'there exists'.
The universal quantifier ∀ states that a predicate P(x) is true for every element in the domain of discourse.
g.text: x: variable being quantified | P(x): predicate depending on x | Domain: set of all possible values for x
"For all natural numbers x, x squared is greater than or equal to zero" — TRUE

g.data-narration: For ∀x P(x) to be TRUE, P(x) must hold for ALL elements. ONE counterexample disproves a universal statement.
g.text: ∀x P(x) is TRUE if P(x) is true for EVERY element in the domain.
∀x P(x) is FALSE if there exists at least ONE counterexample where P(x) is false.
To disprove ∀x P(x), find ONE x where ¬P(x)

g.data-narration: The existential quantifier ∃ states that there is at least one element in the domain for which the predicate is true.
g.text: x: variable being quantified | P(x): predicate depending on x | Domain: set of all possible values for x
"There exists an integer x such that x squared equals four" — TRUE (x = 2 or x = -2)

g.data-narration: For ∃x P(x) to be TRUE, P(x) must be true for at least ONE element. Proving one instance proves the existential.
g.text: ∃x P(x) is TRUE if there exists at least ONE element in the domain where P(x) holds.
∃x P(x) is FALSE if P(x) is false for EVERY element in the domain.
To prove ∃x P(x), find ONE specific x where P(x) is true

g.data-narration: The domain of discourse defines all possible values for variables. The truth of quantified statements depends critically on the domain.
g.text: The domain (or universe of discourse) is the set of all possible values that variables can take.
IMPORTANT: The truth value of quantified statements depends on the domain!

g.data-narration: De Morgan's Laws for quantifiers: negating universal gives existential of negation, and vice versa.
g.text: Negation of Quantified Statements - De Morgan's Laws
Negation of "for all" becomes "there exists" of negation
Negation of "there exists" becomes "for all" of negation
"Not all students passed" = "There exists a student who did not pass"

g.data-narration: With multiple quantifiers, order matters when mixing universal and existential. Same-type quantifiers commute, mixed ones don't.
g.text: Same quantifiers commute: ∀x∀y P(x,y) ≡ ∀y∀x P(x,y) and ∃x∃y P(x,y) ≡ ∃y∃x P(x,y)
Mixed quantifiers DO NOT commute: ∀x∃y P(x,y) ≠ ∃y∀x P(x,y)
∀x∃y (x+y=0): TRUE — for each x, pick y=-x
∃y∀x (x+y=0): FALSE — no single y works for all x

g.data-narration: This diagram shows how different quantifier orders create fundamentally different statements about the same relationship.
g.text: "For every x, there exists a y"
TRUE: pick y = -x for each x
"There exists a y for all x"
Same y must work for ALL x
FALSE: no single y works for every x

g.data-narration: A bound variable is within a quantifier's scope and can be renamed without changing meaning. Free variables depend on external assignment.
g.text: Bound variable: within scope of a quantifier; can be renamed (α-conversion) without changing meaning.
Free variable: not bound by any quantifier; statement truth depends on external assignment to that variable.
x is BOUND (by ∀x) | y is FREE
Truth of statement depends on value of y

g.data-narration: Learn to translate English statements into precise symbolic logic using quantifiers. Words like 'every' and 'some' have specific translations.
g.text: Need different x and y both true

g.data-narration: When negating complex statements, apply De Morgan's laws step by step from the outside quantifier inward to avoid errors.
g.text: "For all integers, if x>0 then x²>0"
"There exists x with x²=2 and x>0"

g.data-narration: Review the key formulas: negation rules, commutation properties, and the critical fact that mixed quantifier order matters.
Avoid these common exam mistakes: forgetting domain, wrong quantifier order, incorrect negation, and confusing 'exists' with 'exists exactly one'.
g.text: 1. Forgetting to specify or consider the domain of discourse
2. Assuming ∀x∃y means the same as ∃y∀x (they're different!)
3. Negating quantifiers incorrectly (not applying De Morgan's laws properly)
4. Confusing ∃!x P(x) ("exists exactly one") with ∃x P(x) ("exists at least one")
5. Treating bound variables as if they affect meaning when renamed

g.data-narration: For exams: always specify the domain, pay attention to quantifier order, use counterexamples wisely, and translate carefully between English and symbols.
